content: I was adopted shortly after birth by Paul and Clara Jobs. They taught me craftsmanship and attention to detail, especially my father, who believed even unseen parts should be built beautifully.
I dropped out of Reed College but continued auditing classes. A calligraphy class deeply influenced my understanding of typography, which later shaped the Macintosh.
Apple was founded in 1976 by Steve Wozniak and me in a garage. We wanted to make computers accessible to individuals, not just corporations.
The Apple II became one of the first successful mass-produced personal computers. It proved that there was a real consumer market for personal computing.
The Macintosh team saw itself as pirates, not the navy. We pushed boundaries and challenged corporate structure to build something insanely great.
The 1984 Super Bowl commercial positioned Macintosh as a rebellion against conformity. It was about empowering individuals.
The Macintosh popularized the graphical user interface and mouse interaction, making computers intuitive and visually driven.
In 1985, after a power struggle with John Sculley and the board, I left Apple. It was devastating but ultimately freeing.
Being forced out of Apple was painful, but it allowed me to enter one of the most creative periods of my life.
I founded NeXT to build advanced workstations for higher education and enterprise markets, focusing on powerful software development tools.
NeXTSTEP was an object-oriented operating system designed to accelerate software innovation and development.
I acquired Pixar in 1986. It combined technology with storytelling, aiming to revolutionize animation.
Toy Story, released in 1995, was the first fully computer-animated feature film. It proved technology and art could merge beautifully.
Focus means saying no to a hundred good ideas. Innovation is not about saying yes to everything.
Design is not just what it looks like and feels like. Design is how it works.
True excellence requires integration of hardware and software. End-to-end control produces superior user experiences.
Having taste matters. You have to pick the right things and reject the mediocre.
Simple can be harder than complex. But once you get there, you can move mountains.
